explanation_md: `a = 5`, `b = 3`. The expression `a * b - a` is `5 * 3 - 5 = 15 - 5 = 10`. `*` has higher precedence than `-`, so multiplication happens first.

options.a: The program prints `0` because integers default-initialize to zero.
options.b: The program does not compile because `x` has no initializer.
options.c: The program compiles but produces undefined behavior — `x` holds a garbage value.
options.d: The program crashes immediately with an error message.

explanation_md: A local `int` variable that is declared but not initialized holds whatever value was in that memory location. Reading it is undefined behavior: the program compiles without error, but the output is unpredictable. This is a classic C++ trap — always initialize your variables.

The first `std::cout` prints the string literal `"n * n"` exactly as written — double quotes make it a string, so no arithmetic happens. The second prints the computed value `6 * 6 = 36`. This is a common mistake: putting an expression inside quotes prints the text, not the result.

prompt_md: Which of the following is the correct order of steps when developing a new program?

options.a: Type `int main()` first, then work out the logic as you code.
options.b: Define the problem in plain English, plan the steps, then write the code one step at a time.
options.c: Write the output statements first, then add the variables needed to produce those outputs.
options.d: Copy a working program and modify it until it does what you want.

explanation_md: Starting with a plain-English description forces you to clarify input and output before writing a single line of C++. Planning the steps next gives you a checklist to follow, making it easy to detect missing logic before you have invested time in code.

options.a: Yes — putting `a * b` in quotes causes C++ to evaluate it at compile time.
options.b: No — putting `a * b` in quotes makes it a string literal; the output would be the text `a * b`, not the computed value.
options.c: Yes — the quotes are required for `std::cout` to call the multiplication operator.
options.d: It does not matter; `std::cout` evaluates expressions inside quotes automatically.

explanation_md: Anything inside double quotes is a string literal — the compiler treats it as raw text to be printed. To print the computed product, the expression must appear outside the quotes. The code shown is already correct.
